metadata.title: Build and Audit a Specification Prompt with Claude Code

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The pandas-import-that-breaks-the-lab-machine versus stdlib csv.reader contrast is exactly the failure I want to design out. I want to build a five-element specification prompt for a CSV-parsing task. Coach me to: (1) write invariants like stdlib-only, utf-8-sig encoding, and empty cells as None; (2) add the context pointer and output contract so two runs come back functionally identical; (3) run the vague version twice to watch it drift between pandas and openpyxl, proving what the spec actually controls.

props.command: The pandas-import-that-breaks-the-lab-machine versus stdlib csv.reader contrast is exactly the failure I want to design out. I want to build a five-element specification prompt for a CSV-parsing task. Coach me to: (1) write invariants like stdlib-only, utf-8-sig encoding, and empty cells as None; (2) add the context pointer and output contract so two runs come back functionally identical; (3) run the vague version twice to watch it drift between pandas and openpyxl, proving what the spec actually controls.
props.segment: Build and Audit a Specification Prompt

beats.narration_text: Two prompts, one minute apart, same model. The specification prompt produces forty lines of correct code. The vague one imports a library that breaks the lab machine.
A specification prompt is not a longer prompt — it is a different kind of thing. It leaves Claude nothing to guess that wasn't authorized.
We run two prompts side by side: a weak vague request, and a five-element specification with invariants, context, output format, and constraints.

props.command:
claude "Write parse_grades(path) -> list[Grade]
  invariants: stdlib only, no pandas
  UTF-8 BOM tolerated
  empty cells -> None not ''
  run: python -m pytest tests/ -q
  do not modify models/grade.py"

beats.narration_text: The specification output uses csv.reader with encoding utf-8-sig, handles empty cells as None, and passes all three tests. The vague output imports pandas and fails on the lab machine.

props.code:
# parse_grades.py — specification-prompt output
import csv, pathlib
from models.grade import Grade

def parse_grades(path) -> list:
    result = []
    with open(path, encoding='utf-8-sig', newline='') as f:
        reader = csv.DictReader(f)
        for row in reader:
            score = row.get('score') or None
            result.append(Grade(
                name=row['name'],
                score=float(score) if score else None
            ))
    return result

beats.narration_text: Two outputs, side by side. The specification version: 42 lines, stdlib only, pytest exit 0. The vague version: 80 lines, pandas import, InstallError on first run. The specification controlled every dimension the vague prompt left open.
We run the weak prompt twice and show the two outputs differ. We run the specification twice and show the outputs are functionally equivalent.

props.command:
claude "Run parse_grades prompt twice.
Are outputs functionally equivalent?
Compare: lib choices, empty-cell handling,
BOM handling across both runs."

beats.narration_text: Weak prompt run 1: pandas. Weak prompt run 2: openpyxl. Both break on lab machines. Specification run 1 and run 2: identical csv.reader implementation. Specification left nothing to draw from training data that didn't know the project.
A specification is not a longer prompt. It is a set of invariants, a context pointer, and an output contract. The difference is not verbosity — it is what Claude is permitted to invent.
Next: catch a package hallucination before it becomes a slopsquatting attack.
